raw_response:
## CC1.4 Readiness Assessment Findings — Q1 2025

| # | Finding Title | Related Document / Section | Why It’s a Gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2025 security awareness training content was launched without required review and CISO approval | **Human Resources Security Policy**, Section 4.3 — Training Content Requirements; **Training Content Review Log**, 2025 row; **Q1 2025 Onboarding Checklist**, Section 3 Notes | Policy requires annual training content to be reviewed and approved by the CISO before the training cycle opens. The 2025 training cycle opened on January 15, 2025 using v2023.1 content, but the 2025 review was not completed until March 28, and there is no evidence of CISO approval before deployment. Sarah Kim later recommended updates, but those were deferred to 2026, meaning personnel completed training that had not been validated as current for 2025 threats, PCI/regulatory changes, or organizational needs. | **Medium** |
| 2 | Key-person dependency caused failure of required training content review process | **Q1 2025 Onboarding Checklist**, Section 3 Notes; **Human Resources Security Policy**, Section 4.3 | The organization relied on the InfoSec Lead to complete the annual training content review. When Mark Stevens departed on December 15, 2024, no interim owner or backup reviewer was assigned before the January 15 training launch. This indicates insufficient succession planning or cross-coverage for a control activity tied to employee competency development. For CC1.4, the gap is not merely procedural; it shows the organization did not maintain sufficient staffing or backup capacity to ensure personnel development controls operated as designed. | **Medium** |
| 3 | Annual security awareness completion requirement was not met by March 31 and leave exceptions are not formally defined | **Human Resources Security Policy**, Section 4.4 — Completion Requirement; **Annual Security Awareness Training Completion Report 2025** | Policy requires 100% annual training completion by March 31. The report shows 17 of 20 employees completed training by the deadline, with three employees marked “Not Completed” due to parental or medical leave. Leave may be a reasonable business exception, but the policy does not define how employees on leave are handled, whether access must be suspended during leave, when make-up training is due upon return, or how exceptions are approved and tracked. Steven Petrov’s access suspension is noted in the AUP tracker, but comparable access status is not documented for Rachel Kim or Tara Okonkwo. | **Low** |
| 4 | Employee who refused the AUP retained full system access | **Human Resources Security Policy**, Sections 5.1 and 5.3 — AUP Signing and Refusal to Sign; **AUP Acknowledgment Tracker**, NXP-6021 Umar Rashid | Policy states personnel must acknowledge the AUP before receiving system credentials, and employees who refuse may not be granted access to company information systems. Umar Rashid refused to sign the AUP, legal review has been open since November 28, 2024, and the tracker states he was granted full system access on October 14, 2024 and access has not been suspended. This is a direct control failure and leaves an employee with active access who has not accepted required obligations for system use, monitoring, and data handling. | **High** |
| 5 | AUP acknowledgment is not effectively gated before access provisioning | **Human Resources Security Policy**, Section 5.1 — AUP Signing; **AUP Acknowledgment Tracker**, NXP-6021 Umar Rashid | The Umar Rashid case indicates that a hiring manager was able to grant full system access before HR verified AUP acknowledgment status. This suggests the AUP process is detective/manual rather than a preventive access prerequisite. For CC1.4, the gap is that personnel security expectations are not consistently enforced before individuals are enabled to perform work using company systems. | **High** |
| 6 | AUP refusal/legal review process lacks timely resolution or compensating control requirements | **Human Resources Security Policy**, Section 5.3 — Refusal to Sign; **AUP Acknowledgment Tracker**, NXP-6021 Umar Rashid | The policy requires HR Business Partner and General Counsel consultation when an employee refuses to sign, but it does not define a required resolution timeframe, interim access restrictions, escalation path, or suspension requirement while legal review is pending. In the documented case, legal review remained open for over four months, and the employee continued to have full system access. The absence of defined timelines and interim controls makes the refusal process ineffective. | **Medium** |
